learningGoal: How to understand that letters stand in for unknown numbers
How to write simple expressions (calculations with letters and numbers) using letters

screens.title: What is "n"?
screens.body:
Mrs Patel has a bag of sweets. She doesn't know how many are inside. She calls the number of sweets **n**.

A letter is just a placeholder — it stands for a number we **don't know yet**. We use it to write maths even when the number is missing!

steps.why: We don't know it yet — that's OK!

steps.text: She adds 5 more sweets to the bag
steps.why: Now we can write an expression

steps.result: A letter + a number = an expression

screens.title: Letters are number placeholders
screens.body:
The expression **n + 5** (a maths phrase using letters and numbers) means "take the unknown number (n) and add 5".

If we later find out that **n = 8**, we can do **substitution** (replacing a letter with its number) to work it out:
**8 + 5 = 13**

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: You know that **n = 12**. Replace the letter with the number, then work it out!

interaction.question: If n = 12, what is n + 5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! Replace n with 12: 12 + 5 = **17**! ✓

screens.title: Letters = unknown numbers!
screens.body: In algebra, a letter is just a **placeholder** for a number we don't know yet.

steps.text: A letter stands for an unknown number
steps.why: n, x, p — any letter works

steps.text: An expression uses letters and numbers
When you know the value, replace the letter!
steps.why: That's called substitution (replacing a letter with a number) ✓

learningGoal: Why 6p means 6 × p, not 6 + p
How to avoid the 'letters as labels' trap

screens.title: What does 6p mean?
screens.body:
Tom says **6p** means **6 + p**.

Is that right? Let's check!

screens.title: It means MULTIPLY!
screens.body:
Tom thought 6p means '6 plus p' but it really means '6 times p'.

In algebra, when a number is written **next to** a letter with no sign, it means **multiplication**.

**6p = 6 × p**

screens.title: If the letter = 6, what is 6p?
screens.body: Remember: **6p** means **6 × p**.

interaction.question: 6p when the letter = 6?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! 6p = 6 × 6 = **36**. Number next to letter = multiply! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 6p means multiply: 6 × 6 = **36**.

screens.title: The golden rule of algebra notation!
screens.body: When a number is written right next to a letter, it means **multiply**. Never add!

steps.text: If you want addition, you write + explicitly

learningGoal: How to replace a letter with a number
How to evaluate (work out the value of) an expression after substituting (putting a number in place of the letter)

screens.title: What is 3x + 4 when x = 5?
screens.body:
Mrs Clarke is working out the total cost of concert tickets. The formula (a rule written as a calculation) is **3x + 4**.

We know **x = 5**. Let's **substitute** — that means replace the letter with the number!

steps.text: Replace the letter with the number
steps.why: This is called substitution (replacing a letter with a number)

screens.title: Replace and calculate!
screens.body:
**Substitution** means replacing a letter with its value. Let's do it!
**Step 1:** Replace **x** with **5** everywhere you see it.
**Step 2:** Work out the answer.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Write, replace, calculate. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first write the expression, then replace the letter, then calculate.

steps.why: Swap the letter for the number

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is **3x + 4** when **x = 7**?

screens.title: The substitution recipe!
screens.body: Substitution is one of the most useful skills in algebra. Just follow these steps:

steps.text: Step 1: Find the letter and its value
Step 2: Replace every letter with the number

learningGoal: Why 3x means 3 × x, not 3x as a two-digit number
How to substitute carefully step by step

screens.title: Jake got 36. Is that right?
screens.body:
Jake substituted **x = 4** into **3x + 2** and got **36**.

That seems too big! What went wrong?

screens.title: It's multiplication, not a two-digit number!
screens.body:
Jake read 3x as the number 34 (thirty-four) instead of 3 × 4 = 12, then added 2 to get 36.

Remember: in algebra, a number next to a letter means **multiply**, not "stick the digits together"!

steps.text: WRONG: "3x" is NOT a two-digit number
steps.why: Jake read 3x as the number 34 (thirty-four) instead of 3 × 4 = 12, then added 2 to get 36

interaction.question: 4x + 3 when x = 5?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! 4x + 3 = **23**. Multiply, don't join! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is **23**. Remember: the number × the letter's value, then add or subtract.

screens.title: Number next to letter = MULTIPLY!
screens.body: Never read 3x as "thirty-x" — it's "3 times x"! This is one of the most common algebra mistakes.

steps.text: 3x means 3 × x, NOT '3 joined with x'
If the answer seems way too big, check this!

learningGoal: How to solve equations with two operations (like + and ×)
How to undo steps in reverse order

screens.title: Solve: 2n + 6 = 18
screens.body:
A sweet shop is selling mystery bags. We need to find **n** in the equation **2n + 6 = 18** (an equation is a maths sentence with an equals sign).

There are two steps to undo. Let's take them one at a time!

screens.title: Undo in reverse order!
screens.body:
The equation does two things to n: first multiply by 2, then add 6.

To undo, go **backwards**: undo the addition first, then undo the multiplication.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Undo the addition first, then undo the multiplication. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — always undo the + or − first, then undo the ×.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Solve **2n + 4 = 14**.

After subtracting 4: 2n = 10.

Now divide by 2. What is n?

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! n = **5**. Check: 2 × 5 + 4 = 14 ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 10 ÷ 2 = **5**. Always divide both sides by the number in front of n.

screens.title: The two-step equation recipe!
screens.body: For any equation like **an + b = c**, undo in reverse order:

steps.text: Step 1: Undo the + or − (subtract or add)
steps.why: Get rid of the constant first
Put your answer back in — it should work ✓

learningGoal: Why doing the steps in the wrong order gives the wrong answer
How to spot the 'wrong order' mistake

screens.title: Rosie got n = 2. Is that right?
screens.body:
Rosie solved **4n + 5 = 29** and got **n = 2**.

Let's check: 4 × 2 + 5 = 13. That's not 29! Something went wrong.

screens.title: Wrong order!
screens.body:
Rosie divided by 4 first (29 ÷ 4 ≈ 7) then subtracted 5 to get 2 — she did the steps in the wrong order.

**Always undo the + or − FIRST**, then undo the ×.

steps.text: WRONG order: divide first, then subtract

screens.title: Now solve this one correctly!
screens.body:
Solve **4n + 3 = 35**.

Subtract 3 first, then divide by 4.

interaction.question: n = 32 ÷ 4 = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! n = **8**. Check: 4 × 8 + 3 = 35 ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 32 ÷ 4 = **8**. Subtract first, then divide.

screens.title: Order matters in equations!
screens.body: Think of an equation like getting dressed: you put on socks THEN shoes. To get undressed, you take off shoes FIRST, then socks. Same with equations — **undo in REVERSE order**!

steps.text: The equation DOES: multiply, then add
To UNDO: subtract first, then divide

learningGoal: How to turn word phrases into algebra
How to spot the operation (add, subtract, multiply or divide) from the words

screens.title: Words → Algebra!
screens.body:
A teacher says: "**double a number and add 5**".

Can we turn those words into an algebra expression? Let's call the unknown number **n**!

screens.title: Spot the key words!
screens.body:
Look for **clue words** that tell you which calculation to use:

- **"double"** or **"twice"** → × 2
- **"three times"** → × 3
- **"add"** or **"plus"** → +
- **"minus"** or **"subtract"** → −

So "**double a number and add 5**" becomes **2n + 5**.

steps.why: Turn each word into a symbol

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
"double a number and add 5" with **n = 10**.

The expression is **2n + 5**. What's the answer?

interaction.question: 2n + 5 when n = 10?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! "double a number and add 5" = **2n + 5** = **25** when n = 10! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 2n + 5 with n = 10 gives **25**.

screens.title: Words to algebra — cheat sheet!
screens.body: Here are the key words that tell you which calculation to use:

learningGoal: How to avoid writing the wrong operation (add, subtract, multiply or divide)
Why reading carefully matters in algebra

screens.title: Is "5n" correct?
screens.body:
Kai turned "**5 more than a number**" into **5n**.

Hmm... does that look right? Read the words carefully!

screens.title: Wrong calculation!
screens.body:
Kai wrote 5n (5 times n) instead of n + 5 — 'more than' means ADD, not multiply.

The correct expression is **n + 5**.

steps.why: Kai wrote 5n (5 times n) instead of n + 5 — 'more than' means ADD, not multiply

screens.title: Which expression is correct?
screens.body: "5 more than a number" — which expression matches?

interaction.question: "5 more than a number" = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! "5 more than a number" = **n + 5**. Read the key words carefully! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! "5 more than a number" = **n + 5**. Kai wrote 5n (5 times n) instead of n + 5 — 'more than' means ADD, not multiply.

screens.title: Read the words carefully!
screens.body: The most common mistake is mixing up "more than" (add) with "times" (multiply). Always underline the key word that tells you which calculation to use!

learningGoal: How to use a function machine that applies operations (like +3 or ×2) step by step
How to work backwards through a function machine

screens.title: What do you notice?
screens.body:
Look at this **function machine**. A number goes in, two steps happen (like +3 or ×2), and a different number comes out!

How does it work?

bodyParts.content: A function machine does each step in order. To undo it, you use the **opposite** — for example, the opposite of + is −.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: **8** goes into the machine. First: **× 3** gives **24**. Then: **+ 4**. What's the output?

interaction.question: What comes out?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 24 then + 4 = **28**.

screens.title: Function machines — forwards and backwards!
screens.body: Go **forwards** when you know the input and want to find the output. Go **backwards** when you know the output and want to find the input!

steps.text: FORWARDS (find the output): follow each step left to right
BACKWARDS (find the input): undo each step right to left
steps.why: Output → undo last step → undo first step → input
Use the opposite to reverse it ✓

learningGoal: How to work backwards through a function machine
How to use opposite operations (like + and −, or × and ÷) to find the input

screens.title: The output is 17. What went in?
screens.body: Oliver's function machine gives an output of **17**. We know what came OUT, but what went IN? We need to go **backwards**!

bodyParts.content: To go backwards, use the **opposite** of each step in **reverse order**:

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Work backwards through the machine using opposite operations. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start at the output and undo the LAST step first.

screens.title: What was the input?
screens.body: Working backwards: 23 − 5 → 18, then ÷ 3 → ?

interaction.question: What was the input?
interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! The input was **6**. Check: 6 × 3 = 18, then + 5 = 23 ✓

screens.title: Going backwards = use opposite operations!
screens.body: Function machines are like equations — you can run them forwards OR backwards!

steps.text: Forwards (find the output): follow each step in order
Backwards (find the input): undo each step in reverse order
steps.why: output → undo last → undo first → input
These are opposite pairs — they undo each other ✓

learningGoal: How to understand that a formula (a rule written as a calculation) is a way of working things out
How to substitute numbers into a formula

screens.title: What's the cost?
screens.body:
A taxi company charges a fixed £2 booking fee plus £3 per mile.

The formula is: **Cost = 2 + 3m** (where m = number of miles)

How much does it cost for **5 miles**?

screens.title: Plug in the number!
screens.body:
Just replace the letter with the number and calculate:

**Cost = 2 + 3m**
With **m = 5**...

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Using the formula **Cost = 2 + 3m** (where m = number of miles) with **m = 8**, what's the cost?

interaction.question: Cost when m = 8?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! With 8 miles, the cost is **£26**! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Substitute m = 8 into Cost = 2 + 3m to get **£26**.

screens.title: Formulae are recipes!
screens.body: A formula is just a **rule** for calculating. Substitute the values and work it out!

steps.text: Read the formula and identify the letter
Replace the letter with the given number

learningGoal: How to use a formula to solve a real problem
How to rearrange a formula to find the unknown

screens.title: How much for 3 hours?
screens.body:
A plumber charges a £20 call-out fee plus £15 per hour.

Formula: **Cost = 20 + 15h**

Let's work out the cost for **3 hours** step by step!

screens.title: Substitute and calculate
screens.body: Replace **h** with **3** and work out each part.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
**Cost = 20 + 15h** with **h = 5**.

The fixed fee is £20. The hourly part is 15 × 5 = 75.

What's the total?

screens.title: Using formulae in real life!
screens.body: Formulae are everywhere — taxi fares, phone bills, plumber charges. The steps are always the same:

steps.text: Identify the formula and the letter

steps.why: Replace the letter with the number

learningGoal: How to use inverse operations — opposite calculations that undo each other (like + and −, or × and ÷)
Why using the wrong inverse gives the wrong answer

screens.title: Noah got x = 23. What went wrong?
screens.body:
Noah tried to solve **x + 8 = 15** and got **x = 23**.

That doesn't check out! Let's see what happened.

screens.title: Use the OPPOSITE!
screens.body:
Noah added 8 to both sides instead of subtracting — he did the same thing again instead of the opposite.

The opposite of **+ 8** is **− 8**. To undo something, you always do the **opposite**!

screens.title: Now solve this one!
screens.body:
**x + 6 = 19**. Use the inverse: **− 6** on both sides.

What is x?

interaction.question: x = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! x = **13**. The opposite of + 6 is − 6! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Use − 6 on both sides to get x = **13**.

screens.title: Opposite pairs!
screens.body: To solve an equation, you must do the **opposite** to undo each step. Here are the pairs:

steps.text: Always do the SAME to BOTH sides

learningGoal: Why equations are like a balance
How to keep an equation balanced by doing the same to both sides

screens.title: An equation is a balance!
screens.body:
Think of **x + 4 = 11** as a balance scale. The left side equals the right side — they're **balanced**!

To find x, we need to get x on its own. But we must keep the balance!

screens.title: Same to both sides!
screens.body:
Whatever you do to one side, you **must** do to the other. That keeps it balanced.

To solve **x + 4 = 11**, do the **opposite** — apply **− 4** to **both** sides:

interaction.sentence: Whatever you do to one side of an equation, you must do the same to the ____ side
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Always do the same to both sides to keep the equation balanced. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — you must do the same thing to the OTHER side to keep it balanced.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Solve **x + 6 = 15** by applying **− 6** to both sides.

What is x?

interaction.question: x = ?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! x = **9**. Balance maintained! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Apply − 6 to both sides: x = **9**.

screens.title: Keep the balance!
screens.body: An equation is a **balance**. The golden rule: whatever you do to one side, do the same to the other!

steps.text: An equation means: left side = right side
Do the SAME thing to BOTH sides
steps.why: Add 5 to left? Add 5 to right too!

steps.text: Use the OPPOSITE to isolate x
steps.why: If it says +5, do −5 to get x on its own ✓

learningGoal: How to use BIDMAS (Brackets, Indices, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction) to decide which calculations to do first
How to recognise that brackets change which part you do first

screens.title: What is 3 + 4 × 2?
screens.body:
Miss Clarke says **3 + 4 × 2 = 14**.

But that's wrong! The correct answer is **11**. Why? Because of a rule called **BIDMAS (Brackets, Indices, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction)**!

screens.title: BIDMAS tells you the order!
screens.body:
**BIDMAS** tells you which calculations to do first:

**B**rackets → **I**ndices → **D**ivision → **M**ultiplication → **A**ddition → **S**ubtraction

Multiplication and division come **before** addition and subtraction!

So for **3 + 4 × 2**: multiplication first: 4 × 2 = 8, then 3 + 8 = 11.

interaction.sentence: In BIDMAS, the B stands for ____ — always do these first
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! B = Brackets — always calculate what's inside brackets first. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — B stands for Brackets. Always do brackets first!

screens.title: Apply BIDMAS!
screens.body:
What is **8 − 3 × 2** using BIDMAS?

Remember: × and ÷ come before + and −!

interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! multiplication first: 3 × 2 = 6, then 8 − 6 = 2. BIDMAS in action! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Remember BIDMAS: multiplication first: 3 × 2 = 6, then 8 − 6 = 2. Answer: **2**.

screens.title: BIDMAS — remember the order!
screens.body: BIDMAS keeps everyone's maths consistent. Without it, the same expression could give different answers!

steps.why: Whatever's in brackets gets done first
This is the bit most people forget!

steps.text: Left to right when they're at the same level

learningGoal: How to see that brackets change the answer
Why missing brackets leads to errors

screens.title: Brackets make a difference!
screens.body:
Tom needed to calculate **(3 + 5) × 2** but wrote it as **3 + 5 × 2** (without brackets).

Are the answers the same?

screens.title: NOT the same!
screens.body:
Tom forgot the brackets and did 5 × 2 first (= 10), then 3 + 10 = 13, instead of (3 + 5) × 2 = 8 × 2 = 16.

Brackets tell you to do that part **first**, before anything else. Without them, BIDMAS (Brackets, Indices, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction) applies and the order changes!

screens.title: Now try this one!
screens.body: Calculate **(4 + 3) × 5** — do the brackets first!

interaction.correctFeedback: Superstar! (4 + 3) × 5 = **35**. Brackets change everything! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Do the brackets first: (4 + 3) × 5 = **35**.

screens.title: Brackets always come first!
screens.body: If you see brackets, do that part FIRST. Brackets override everything else in BIDMAS.

steps.text: Always write brackets when you need them!
